Title: Platform Foundation
Description: Establish the complete development infrastructure, cloud hosting environment, authentication system, shared TypeScript packages, and API gateway that ALL subsequent service epics depend on. This epic covers Sprints 0 and 1 (Weeks 1–4). No end-user features are delivered; the output is a fully operational development and staging environment with a working authentication and authorisation flow. Completion of this epic is the hard gate for starting Epic 2 onwards. Reference: Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 0 and Sprint 1.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Every engineer can clone the repo and run docker compose up in under 2 minutes
- GKE Autopilot staging cluster operational with Istio mTLS enforced
- All Tier 0 data stores running in staging (Postgres, Redis, NATS, Neo4j, Elasticsearch, Temporal, S3)
- Auth Service end-to-end: Google OAuth login returns valid RS256 JWT
- Apollo Router federated gateway running; JWT validation enforced at gateway
- All shared packages (ast, events, proto, common, db) compile with zero TypeScript errors
- CI pipeline green on main branch

Title: Development Environment and Monorepo Setup
Description: Set up the monorepo scaffold (pnpm workspaces + Turborepo + Cargo workspace), Docker Compose local development environment, and the GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline. The goal is to give every engineer a zero-friction onboarding experience: clone, install, and the entire local stack is running. CI enforces code quality on every pull request. This feature is the first thing built in Sprint 0 because every other task in the project depends on a working build system. Reference: Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 0 tasks 1–2.
Acceptance Criteria:
- pnpm install completes without errors across macOS, Windows WSL2, and Linux
- docker compose up starts Postgres, Redis, NATS, and Neo4j locally in under 2 minutes
- pnpm build runs successfully with Turborepo caching across all packages and services
- GitHub Actions CI triggers on every PR: lint, typecheck, test, and Docker build all pass
- ESLint import restriction rule is enforced: services may not import from other services
- Turborepo affected mode ensures only changed packages are rebuilt in CI
Parent: Platform Foundation

Title: As a developer I can clone the repo and start the full local development stack with a single docker compose up command in under 2 minutes
Description: A new engineer joining the team should be able to be productive on day one without manual dependency installation or environment configuration. The local stack must include all Tier 0 services (Postgres, Redis, NATS, Neo4j) so service development can start immediately. The monorepo structure must enforce workspace-level dependency management and build caching from day one to prevent the slow CI times that plague large TypeScript monorepos. Reference: Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 0 task — Create monorepo.
Acceptance Criteria:
- pnpm install at repo root installs all workspace dependencies without errors
- docker compose up starts: Postgres 16, Redis 7, NATS 2.10 with JetStream, Neo4j 5, Elasticsearch 8.9 in under 2 minutes on a standard dev machine (8-core, 16 GB RAM)
- Each container has a health check; docker compose up --wait returns only when all services are healthy
- .env.example documents every required environment variable with placeholder values and inline comments
- pnpm build runs the Turborepo build pipeline with caching; second run uses cache and completes in under 30 seconds
- Cargo workspace builds successfully (no Rust code yet, but workspace.toml is valid and cargo check passes)
Parent: Development Environment and Monorepo Setup

Title: Initialize monorepo root structure with pnpm workspaces Turborepo and Cargo workspace
Description: Create the top-level monorepo configuration files. pnpm-workspace.yaml must declare all workspace globs. turbo.json must define the full pipeline topology (build depends on ^build, test depends on build, lint has no deps, typecheck has no deps). Cargo.toml must include the Tauri on-set app as a workspace member. The directory skeleton (packages/, services/, workers/, apps/, infra/) must be created with placeholder README files. This is the first task in the project and blocks everything else.
Parent: As a developer I can clone the repo and start the full local development stack with a single docker compose up command in under 2 minutes

Title: Write docker-compose.yml for local development stack
Description: Create docker-compose.yml at repo root with the following named services: postgres (image: postgres:16, port 5432, volume for data persistence, POSTGRES_PASSWORD from env), redis (image: redis:7-alpine, port 6379), nats (image: nats:2.10-alpine, port 4222 + 8222 management, JetStream enabled via -js flag), neo4j (image: neo4j:5-community, ports 7474 + 7687, NEO4J_AUTH from env), elasticsearch (image: elasticsearch:8.9.0, port 9200, single-node discovery, xpack.security.enabled=false for dev). All services must have healthcheck definitions. Add a depends_on chain so service containers wait for healthy infrastructure.
Parent: As a developer I can clone the repo and start the full local development stack with a single docker compose up command in under 2 minutes

Title: Write .env.example with all required environment variable placeholders
Description: Create .env.example at repo root documenting every environment variable used across all services and packages. Each variable must have: a descriptive inline comment, a safe placeholder value (never a real credential), and a note if it is required vs optional. Categories to cover: database connection strings (Postgres, Redis, Neo4j, Elasticsearch), cloud service credentials (S3, Temporal, Frame.io), JWT signing (RS256 private key path), feature flags (UNLEASH_URL), AI API keys (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY). Create a separate .env.local.example for Docker Compose local overrides.
Parent: As a developer I can clone the repo and start the full local development stack with a single docker compose up command in under 2 minutes

Title: Configure Turborepo pipeline with correct task dependency topology and remote caching
Description: Write turbo.json at repo root defining the build pipeline. Tasks: build (outputs: dist/**, .next/**, dependsOn: [^build]), test (dependsOn: [build], outputs: coverage/**), lint (no dependsOn, no outputs), typecheck (no dependsOn, no outputs). Configure pipeline.dev for watch mode. Set up Turborepo remote cache pointing to Vercel Remote Cache (or self-hosted if budget constraints apply — decision to be made Sprint 0 week 1). Document cache keys and explain why cross-service imports are prohibited in pipeline topology comments.
Parent: As a developer I can clone the repo and start the full local development stack with a single docker compose up command in under 2 minutes

Title: Configure Cargo workspace root for Tauri Rust backend
Description: Create Cargo.toml at repo root as a Cargo workspace with members = [apps/onset/src-tauri]. Set rust-edition to 2021. Add workspace-level lints configuration (clippy::all = warn). Create apps/onset/src-tauri/Cargo.toml with placeholder tauri dependency. Verify cargo check passes on empty workspace. This unblocks Sprint 10 (Tauri on-set app) without requiring Rust work now.
Parent: As a developer I can clone the repo and start the full local development stack with a single docker compose up command in under 2 minutes

Title: As a developer every pull request is validated by a CI pipeline that enforces lint typecheck tests and Docker build before merge
Description: Engineers should never merge broken code. The CI pipeline must catch TypeScript errors, import violations, test failures, and unbuildable Docker images before they reach main. Turborepo affected mode ensures CI only runs against packages changed in the PR, keeping CI times under 10 minutes even as the codebase grows. The import restriction ESLint rule enforces the architectural constraint that services must never import from other services — cross-service communication goes through gRPC only. Reference: Wiki/16-system-design.md dependency rule, Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 0.
Acceptance Criteria:
- GitHub Actions CI workflow triggers on every pull request to main
- CI runs in this order: lint (ESLint), typecheck (tsc --noEmit), test (Vitest), build (Docker)
- Turborepo affected mode: only packages and services changed in the PR are linted, typechecked, and tested
- ESLint catches and fails CI when a service imports directly from another service (import/no-restricted-paths rule)
- Docker images are built for all changed services but not pushed to registry on PRs
- Docker images are built and pushed to GCR on merge to main with tag = git commit SHA
- CI completes in under 10 minutes for a typical single-service PR
Parent: Development Environment and Monorepo Setup

Title: Create GitHub Actions CI workflow for lint typecheck test and Docker build
Description: Create .github/workflows/ci.yml. Triggers: pull_request (branches: [main]), push (branches: [main]). Jobs: (1) lint-typecheck: checkout, pnpm install with frozen lockfile, run pnpm turbo lint typecheck --filter=...[HEAD~1] (affected mode). (2) test: checkout, pnpm install, run pnpm turbo test --filter=...[HEAD~1]. (3) docker-build: checkout, set up Docker Buildx, build Docker image for each service changed in PR (use git diff to determine changed services), tag with PR SHA. On push to main: add docker-push job that pushes images to GCR with SHA tag and latest tag.
Parent: As a developer every pull request is validated by a CI pipeline that enforces lint typecheck tests and Docker build before merge

Title: Configure ESLint with import restriction rules to prevent cross-service imports
Description: Create .eslintrc.js (or eslint.config.js for flat config) at repo root. Install: eslint, @typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin, @typescript-eslint/parser, eslint-plugin-import. Add rule: import/no-restricted-paths with patterns that prevent services/* from importing from other services/*. Pattern: { from: services/*, to: services/*, message: Cross-service imports are forbidden. Use gRPC APIs instead. } Also add rules: no-console (warn), @typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any (warn), @typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars (error). Create .eslintignore for dist/ and generated/ directories.
Parent: As a developer every pull request is validated by a CI pipeline that enforces lint typecheck tests and Docker build before merge

Title: Configure Vitest workspace for unit testing across all packages and services
Description: Create vitest.workspace.ts at repo root referencing all packages/* and services/* directories. Each package/service will have its own vitest.config.ts when created — this workspace file discovers them. Configure coverage provider (v8), coverage thresholds (branches: 60%, lines: 70% — baseline, not production target), and reporters (text, html, json for CI). Add test script to each package.json: vitest run. Wire test task into Turborepo pipeline. Verify vitest passes on empty test suites.
Parent: As a developer every pull request is validated by a CI pipeline that enforces lint typecheck tests and Docker build before merge

Title: Create multi-stage Dockerfile template for TypeScript services
Description: Create infra/docker/Dockerfile.service as the canonical multi-stage template for all Node.js TypeScript services. Stages: (1) deps: node:20-alpine, copy package.json + pnpm-lock.yaml, pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --prod=false. (2) build: copy source, run pnpm turbo build --filter=<service>. (3) runtime: node:20-alpine, copy built output from build stage, copy node_modules from deps stage (prune dev deps), set NODE_ENV=production, add non-root user (node:node), HEALTHCHECK, CMD [node, dist/index.js]. Document how each service customises the template via build args.
Parent: As a developer every pull request is validated by a CI pipeline that enforces lint typecheck tests and Docker build before merge

Title: Configure GCR image registry and push workflow for main branch merges
Description: Create .github/workflows/release.yml triggered on push to main. Steps: authenticate to GCP using Workload Identity Federation (no service account keys), configure Docker to use gcloud as credential helper, build and push each service image to GCR (us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/scriptos/services/<service-name>) tagged with both git-sha and latest. Create the GCR repository via Terraform (infra/terraform/registry.tf). Document the image naming convention and retention policy (keep last 10 images per service).
Parent: As a developer every pull request is validated by a CI pipeline that enforces lint typecheck tests and Docker build before merge

Title: Cloud Infrastructure Provisioning
Description: Provision all cloud infrastructure required for the staging environment: GKE Autopilot cluster, Istio service mesh, observability stack (OpenTelemetry + Prometheus + Grafana + Loki), all data stores (PostgreSQL, Redis, NATS, Neo4j, Elasticsearch, Temporal, S3), and secret management. All infrastructure is defined as code (Terraform + Helm) and reproducible from scratch. This feature must be complete before any service can be deployed to staging. Reference: Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 0, Wiki/12-infrastructure.md.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Terraform apply provisions the full staging stack from scratch without errors
- GKE Autopilot cluster running with kubectl get nodes returning 3+ nodes
- Istio mTLS enforced between all service namespaces
- All data stores healthy and accessible from within the cluster
- OpenTelemetry traces visible in Grafana/Jaeger for a hello-world service
- All secrets stored in GCP Secret Manager and mounted via External Secrets Operator
Parent: Platform Foundation

Title: As a platform engineer I can run a single Terraform apply to provision the complete GKE staging cluster with service mesh and observability stack
Description: Infrastructure as code ensures the staging environment is reproducible, auditable, and portable. A single Terraform apply should provision everything from scratch — no manual console clicks. The observability stack must be running before any service is deployed so traces and metrics are captured from day one. Istio mTLS must be enforced at cluster level to match the production security posture. Reference: Wiki/12-infrastructure.md, ADR-026 (GKE Autopilot), Wiki/11-security-compliance.md.
Acceptance Criteria:
- terraform apply -target=module.gke succeeds; cluster appears in GCP console
- kubectl get nodes returns 3+ Autopilot nodes in Ready state
- istioctl analyze returns no errors or warnings on the cluster
- Kiali dashboard (or Grafana Istio dashboard) shows mTLS mode: STRICT for all namespaces
- Prometheus scrapes metrics; test metric scriptos_hello_requests_total visible in Grafana
- OpenTelemetry collector receives trace spans; spans visible in Jaeger/Tempo UI
- Loki receives logs from all pods; log query for a test pod returns results
- External Secrets Operator syncs a test secret from GCP Secret Manager to Kubernetes Secret
Parent: Cloud Infrastructure Provisioning

Title: Write Terraform configuration for GKE Autopilot cluster
Description: Create infra/terraform/gke.tf. Provider: google (region us-central1, project var.project_id). Resource: google_container_cluster with enable_autopilot = true, release_channel = REGULAR, network/subnetwork from VPC module, workload_identity_config enabled. Create infra/terraform/vpc.tf for the VPC and subnet. Create infra/terraform/variables.tf with: project_id, region, environment (staging/production). Create infra/terraform/outputs.tf exporting cluster endpoint, CA cert, and kubeconfig generation command. Add to CI: terraform fmt check on PR.
Parent: As a platform engineer I can run a single Terraform apply to provision the complete GKE staging cluster with service mesh and observability stack

Title: Write Helm values and installation script for Istio service mesh
Description: Create infra/helm/istio/ directory. Write install-istio.sh: use istioctl install with IstioOperator manifest. Write infra/helm/istio/operator.yaml: profile: default, components.pilot.k8s.resources (set appropriate limits for Autopilot), meshConfig.accessLogFile: /dev/stdout, meshConfig.enableTracing: true, meshConfig.defaultConfig.tracing.zipkin.address pointing to OTel collector. Create PeerAuthentication resource (infra/k8s/istio/peer-auth.yaml) with STRICT mTLS mode for the scriptos namespace. Create AuthorizationPolicy allowing service-to-service traffic within mesh.
Parent: As a platform engineer I can run a single Terraform apply to provision the complete GKE staging cluster with service mesh and observability stack

Title: Deploy OpenTelemetry collector and configure trace and metric pipelines
Description: Create infra/k8s/otel/collector.yaml: OpenTelemetry Collector DaemonSet with OTLP gRPC receiver (port 4317), OTLP HTTP receiver (port 4318). Processors: batch, memory_limiter. Exporters: (1) Prometheus exporter for metrics, (2) Jaeger/Tempo exporter for traces, (3) Loki exporter for logs. Create ConfigMap with otel-collector.yaml config. Write Kubernetes Service exposing ports 4317 and 4318. Create namespace-level annotation to auto-inject OTel SDK env vars into all pods (OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT pointing to collector service).
Parent: As a platform engineer I can run a single Terraform apply to provision the complete GKE staging cluster with service mesh and observability stack

Title: Deploy kube-prometheus-stack for Prometheus and Grafana
Description: Add kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart to infra/helm/kube-prometheus/. Write values.yaml: grafana.adminPassword from Secret, prometheus.prometheusSpec.storageSpec (10Gi PVC for staging), grafana.dashboardProviders pointing to infra/helm/kube-prometheus/dashboards/. Add pre-built dashboards for: GKE node metrics, NATS JetStream, Postgres (postgres-exporter), Redis, Istio service mesh. Create Grafana Ingress with TLS (staging.scriptos.internal). Document: how to add new dashboards via ConfigMap.
Parent: As a platform engineer I can run a single Terraform apply to provision the complete GKE staging cluster with service mesh and observability stack

Title: Deploy Loki and Promtail for log aggregation
Description: Add Loki + Promtail Helm chart to infra/helm/loki/. Use Loki monolithic mode (single binary) for staging (saves cost; distributed mode for production in Sprint 18). Configure Promtail DaemonSet to scrape all pod logs and add Kubernetes labels (namespace, pod, container, app) as Loki stream labels. Configure Loki retention: 14 days for staging. Add Loki as a data source in Grafana. Create sample LogQL queries for common patterns (error rate by service, slow gRPC calls). Document: how to query logs in Grafana.
Parent: As a platform engineer I can run a single Terraform apply to provision the complete GKE staging cluster with service mesh and observability stack

Title: Configure External Secrets Operator for GCP Secret Manager integration
Description: Install External Secrets Operator (ESO) via Helm into the scriptos namespace. Create ClusterSecretStore resource pointing to GCP Secret Manager using Workload Identity (no service account key files). Create SecretStore for each namespace. Create ExternalSecret resources for: postgres-connection-string, redis-url, neo4j-uri, neo4j-password, elasticsearch-api-key, temporal-tls-cert, temporal-tls-key, jwt-rs256-private-key, anthropic-api-key, openai-api-key, s3-access-key, s3-secret-key. Refresh interval: 1h. Document: how to add new secrets to Secret Manager and reference in pods.
Parent: As a platform engineer I can run a single Terraform apply to provision the complete GKE staging cluster with service mesh and observability stack

Title: As a platform engineer all data stores and messaging infrastructure are provisioned in staging and accessible from service pods
Description: The build order for services is blocked until all data stores are available. Every service has hard dependencies on one or more data stores. A service that starts before its database is ready will crash-loop. This user story ensures all Tier 0 infrastructure (per the Service Bring-Up Dependency Order in wiki/15) is running before Sprint 1 service development begins. Reference: Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 0 tasks, Wiki/12-infrastructure.md.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Cloud SQL Postgres 16 is accessible from GKE via private IP; psql connection succeeds from within cluster
- Redis is accessible; redis-cli ping returns PONG from a test pod
- NATS JetStream is accessible; nats stream ls returns empty list (no streams yet); JetStream enabled
- Neo4j Aura is provisioned; Cypher query via Bolt protocol from test pod returns server version
- Elasticsearch 8.9 cluster health returns green; index creation API call succeeds
- Temporal Cloud namespace scriptos is active; temporal workflow list returns empty (no workflows yet)
- S3 buckets exist; test PutObject and GetObject from GKE workload identity succeeds
- Atlas CLI runs atlas schema apply on packages/db against empty Postgres without errors
Parent: Cloud Infrastructure Provisioning

Title: Provision Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 instance via Terraform
Description: Create infra/terraform/postgres.tf. Resource: google_sql_database_instance with database_version = POSTGRES_16, settings.tier = db-g1-small (staging), settings.ip_configuration.ipv4_enabled = false (private IP only), settings.ip_configuration.private_network pointing to VPC, settings.backup_configuration enabled (daily, 7-day retention for staging). Create google_sql_user for the application user. Store connection string in Secret Manager via google_secret_manager_secret resource. Configure Cloud SQL Auth Proxy as a sidecar in service pods (add annotation to Deployment template).
Parent: As a platform engineer all data stores and messaging infrastructure are provisioned in staging and accessible from service pods

Title: Provision Redis Memorystore Standard tier and verify connectivity from GKE
Description: Create infra/terraform/redis.tf. Resource: google_redis_instance with tier = STANDARD_HA (for staging; 1 GB memory), connect_mode = PRIVATE_SERVICE_ACCESS, redis_version = REDIS_7_0. Store Redis URL (redis://<ip>:6379) in Secret Manager. Create a verification Job in Kubernetes that runs redis-cli ping and logs the result. Document: Redis is used for (1) CRDT doc state cache, (2) session cache, (3) pub/sub for awareness, (4) rate limiting via Lua scripts. Reference: Wiki/07-workflow-orchestration.md.
Parent: As a platform engineer all data stores and messaging infrastructure are provisioned in staging and accessible from service pods

Title: Deploy NATS JetStream 3-node cluster on GKE via Helm
Description: Create infra/helm/nats/values.yaml using the official NATS Helm chart. Configure: replicas: 3, jetstream.enabled: true, jetstream.fileStore.enabled: true with 5Gi PVC per node, cluster.enabled: true, natsbox.enabled: true (for debugging). Expose client port 4222 as ClusterIP service. Configure NATS accounts for service isolation (future: per-service credentials). Store NATS URL in Secret Manager. Write a verification script that creates a test JetStream stream and publishes/consumes a message. Document: all NATS subjects used in ScriptOS (script.checkpointed, breakdown.updated, supervisor.*) in packages/events/subjects.ts.
Parent: As a platform engineer all data stores and messaging infrastructure are provisioned in staging and accessible from service pods

Title: Provision Neo4j Aura instance and store credentials in Secret Manager
Description: Provision Neo4j Aura Professional instance via the Neo4j Aura console (not Terraform — Aura does not have a Terraform provider as of 2026). Region: us-central1 (or nearest available). Instance size: 1 GB RAM (staging). Store in GCP Secret Manager: NEO4J_URI (bolt+s://...), NEO4J_USERNAME, NEO4J_PASSWORD. Create a verification Kubernetes Job that runs a Cypher query (RETURN 1 AS ok) and logs the result. Document: when to evaluate switching to self-hosted Neo4j on GKE (trigger: Aura costs exceed $800/month per risk register in wiki/15). Reference: ADR-002.
Parent: As a platform engineer all data stores and messaging infrastructure are provisioned in staging and accessible from service pods

Title: Provision Elasticsearch 8.9 cluster on Elastic Cloud and store API key in Secret Manager
Description: Provision Elasticsearch 8.9+ cluster on Elastic Cloud (not self-hosted for staging — reduces operational burden). Configuration: 1 zone, 4 GB RAM hot tier (staging). Enable: machine learning (for future vector inference), APM integration optional. Create an API key scoped to the scriptos index pattern. Store in Secret Manager: ELASTICSEARCH_URL, ELASTICSEARCH_API_KEY. Create a verification Job that calls the /_cluster/health endpoint and asserts status: green. Document: when to evaluate self-hosted Elasticsearch (trigger: egress costs or data residency requirement). Reference: ADR-005, Wiki/10-search.md.
Parent: As a platform engineer all data stores and messaging infrastructure are provisioned in staging and accessible from service pods

Title: Provision Temporal Cloud namespace and store TLS certificates in Secret Manager
Description: Sign up for Temporal Cloud and provision a namespace named scriptos-staging in us-central1. Download: CA certificate, client certificate, client private key for mTLS connection. Store in GCP Secret Manager: TEMPORAL_ADDRESS (e.g. scriptos-staging.a1b2c.tmprl.cloud:7233), TEMPORAL_NAMESPACE, TEMPORAL_TLS_CERT (base64), TEMPORAL_TLS_KEY (base64). Create a test connection from a pod using the Temporal TypeScript SDK: temporalClient.workflowService.getSystemInfo() must succeed. Document: Temporal task queues used in ScriptOS — PUBLISH_SAGA, IMPORT_SAGA, CALL_SHEET, REVISION_DIST. Reference: ADR-004, Wiki/07-workflow-orchestration.md.
Parent: As a platform engineer all data stores and messaging infrastructure are provisioned in staging and accessible from service pods

Title: Create S3 buckets with IAM roles for GKE Workload Identity via Terraform
Description: Create infra/terraform/s3.tf (AWS provider). Resources: aws_s3_bucket for scriptos-media-staging, scriptos-exports-staging, scriptos-backups-staging. Bucket configs: versioning enabled, server-side encryption (AES-256), public access blocked. CORS policy on scriptos-media (allow PUT from app domain for direct upload). Lifecycle rule on scriptos-backups: transition to Glacier after 30 days. Create IAM role with policies: s3:PutObject + s3:GetObject on media bucket, s3:PutObject on exports bucket. Use GCP Workload Identity Federation to allow GKE pods to assume this AWS role without access keys. Store role ARN and bucket names in Secret Manager.
Parent: As a platform engineer all data stores and messaging infrastructure are provisioned in staging and accessible from service pods

Title: Install Atlas CLI and initialize packages/db Atlas project with empty migration baseline
Description: Install Atlas CLI globally in the CI Docker image and document installation for local dev. In packages/db/, create: atlas.hcl (project config referencing each service schema file), a schemas/ directory. Run atlas schema inspect on empty Postgres and save as baseline. Verify atlas schema apply runs without errors on a fresh Postgres instance. Write the first actual schema: auth.hcl containing auth.users, auth.organizations, auth.sessions, auth.refresh_tokens tables (column definitions per wiki/03-data-model.md auth tables). Verify apply creates all tables and indexes. Document: Atlas HCL format and conventions used in this project.
Parent: As a platform engineer all data stores and messaging infrastructure are provisioned in staging and accessible from service pods

Title: Authentication Service
Description: Build the Auth Service: OIDC/SAML SSO login (Google, Okta), JWT issuance (RS256, 15-min access + 30-day refresh), 7-role RBAC, row-level security pattern, and GraphQL subgraph. This is the first microservice deployed to staging. Every other service in the platform depends on the Auth Service for identity context. The Auth Service issues the JWT that the HTTP Gateway validates — no other service touches raw credentials. Reference: Wiki/16-system-design.md Auth Service, Wiki/11-security-compliance.md, Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 1.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Google OAuth login: browser redirects to Google, authorises, callback returns JWT to client
- JWT contains: sub (user UUID), org (org UUID), roles (array), tier (pricing tier), iat, exp
- RS256 JWT validates correctly against public key exposed at /auth/.well-known/jwks.json
- Refresh token rotation works: POST /auth/refresh returns new access + refresh; old refresh invalidated
- 7 RBAC roles defined; X-Roles header injected at gateway; producer-only endpoint returns 403 for writer role
- Auth Service GraphQL subgraph: query { me { id email roles } } returns correct user data through Apollo Router
- Atlas migration for auth.* tables applies cleanly to fresh Postgres
Parent: Platform Foundation

Title: As a user I can sign in with my Google Workspace or Okta SSO account and receive a secure JWT that grants access to ScriptOS
Description: Writers, producers, supervisors, and editors all log in via their studio's identity provider — they should not have a separate ScriptOS password. Google Workspace covers indie and small studio users; Okta covers enterprise studios with managed identity. The JWT must be short-lived (15 min) to limit exposure if leaked; the refresh token enables seamless session renewal without re-authentication. All authentication events must be logged to the immutable audit log for SOC 2 compliance. Reference: Wiki/11-security-compliance.md, ADR (Auth)
Acceptance Criteria:
- GET /auth/google/login returns HTTP 302 redirect to Google OAuth consent screen
- POST /auth/google/callback with valid code creates user record if new; returns access_token (15-min RS256 JWT) and sets refresh_token httpOnly cookie (30-day)
- GET /auth/me with valid Bearer token returns { id, email, name, orgId, roles, tier }
- POST /auth/refresh with valid refresh cookie returns new access_token; old refresh token is invalidated (rotation)
- Expired access token returns 401 with WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error=invalid_token
- Okta SAML: SP-initiated login flow works end-to-end (test with Okta developer account)
- Every login attempt (success or failure) written to audit log with timestamp, IP, user agent
Parent: Authentication Service

Title: Define Atlas schema for auth database tables
Description: Create packages/db/schemas/auth.hcl with HCL schema definitions for all auth tables. Tables required: auth.users (id UUID PK, email VARCHAR UNIQUE NOT NULL, name VARCHAR, google_sub VARCHAR UNIQUE, okta_sub VARCHAR UNIQUE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_login_at TIMESTAMPTZ, is_active BOOL DEFAULT true), auth.organizations (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR NOT NULL, tier VARCHAR NOT NULL CHECK IN (indie, professional, studio), created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), auth.organization_members (org_id UUID FK organizations, user_id UUID FK users, role VARCHAR NOT NULL, joined_at TIMESTAMPTZ, PRIMARY KEY (org_id, user_id)), auth.refresh_tokens (id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK users, token_hash VARCHAR UNIQUE NOT NULL, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL, revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). Add indexes: users(email), users(google_sub), refresh_tokens(token_hash). Run atlas schema apply; verify tables and indexes created.
Parent: As a user I can sign in with my Google Workspace or Okta SSO account and receive a secure JWT that grants access to ScriptOS

Title: Implement OIDC provider integration for Google and Okta using openid-client
Description: In services/auth/src/providers/: create google.provider.ts and okta.provider.ts using the openid-client npm library. Google: use discovery URL accounts.google.com/.well-known/openid-configuration, scope: openid email profile. Okta: use organisation-specific discovery URL, scope: openid email profile groups. Both providers: implement (1) buildAuthorizationUrl(state, nonce) returning redirect URL, (2) exchangeCode(callbackParams) returning UserInfo. Store client_id and client_secret in env vars (from Secret Manager via ESO). Write unit tests using nock to mock OIDC discovery and token endpoints.
Parent: As a user I can sign in with my Google Workspace or Okta SSO account and receive a secure JWT that grants access to ScriptOS

Title: Implement JWT signing service with RS256 key pair managed via Secret Manager
Description: Create services/auth/src/jwt.service.ts. On startup: load RS256 private key PEM from env var JWT_PRIVATE_KEY (sourced from Secret Manager). Derive public key from private key. Expose /auth/.well-known/jwks.json endpoint serving the public key as a JWK set (kid: key-1, alg: RS256, use: sig). signAccessToken(payload): sign with private key, exp: 15 minutes, iss: https://auth.scriptos.com. signRefreshToken(userId): random 256-bit token, store SHA-256 hash in auth.refresh_tokens with 30-day expiry. verifyAccessToken(token): verify with public key, check exp and iss. Write unit tests for sign, verify, and expiry.
Parent: As a user I can sign in with my Google Workspace or Okta SSO account and receive a secure JWT that grants access to ScriptOS

Title: Implement OAuth callback token issuance and refresh token rotation REST endpoints
Description: Create services/auth/src/routes/auth.routes.ts. Implement: GET /auth/google/login (redirect to Google), GET /auth/google/callback (exchange code, upsert user, issue JWT + refresh), GET /auth/okta/login (redirect to Okta), GET /auth/okta/callback, GET /auth/me (validate Bearer token, return user), POST /auth/refresh (validate refresh cookie, rotate token, return new access JWT), POST /auth/logout (revoke refresh token, clear cookie). All endpoints write to audit log. Use express-async-errors for error handling. Configure express-rate-limit on /auth/login routes (10 attempts per 15 min per IP) to prevent brute force.
Parent: As a user I can sign in with my Google Workspace or Okta SSO account and receive a secure JWT that grants access to ScriptOS

Title: Write integration tests for Auth Service covering full OAuth flow token validation and refresh
Description: Create services/auth/tests/integration/auth.test.ts using Vitest + Supertest. Test cases: (1) Full Google OAuth flow with mocked OIDC provider (use nock): login URL generation → callback with code → JWT returned → me endpoint returns user. (2) Token validation: valid JWT → 200, expired JWT → 401, tampered JWT → 401, missing JWT → 401. (3) Refresh token rotation: valid refresh cookie → new access token → old refresh invalidated → second use of old refresh → 401. (4) Rate limiting: 11 login attempts in 15 min → 429. Run against real Postgres (test DB) using testcontainers.
Parent: As a user I can sign in with my Google Workspace or Okta SSO account and receive a secure JWT that grants access to ScriptOS

Title: Deploy Auth Service Docker image to GKE staging with health check and readiness probe
Description: Build Auth Service Docker image using infra/docker/Dockerfile.service template. Create infra/k8s/auth/deployment.yaml: 2 replicas, resource limits (256m CPU, 256Mi RAM for staging), env vars from ExternalSecret resources, readinessProbe on GET /health (must return 200 before traffic routed), livenessProbe on GET /health (restart if 3 consecutive failures). Create infra/k8s/auth/service.yaml (ClusterIP on port 80 → container 3000). Create Istio VirtualService routing /auth/* to Auth Service. Apply to staging cluster; verify pod Running and READY. Add Auth Service deployment to CI release workflow.
Parent: As a user I can sign in with my Google Workspace or Okta SSO account and receive a secure JWT that grants access to ScriptOS

Title: As a platform engineer all API requests carry RBAC role claims so downstream services can enforce authorisation without querying the database
Description: Downstream services must never make a database call to check if a user is allowed to perform an action — this creates N+1 auth DB queries at scale. The JWT carries the user's roles and org ID. The HTTP Gateway validates the JWT and injects X-User-ID, X-Org-ID, X-Roles, X-Tier headers into every forwarded request. Services read these headers and apply authorization logic. Row-level security in Postgres uses app.org_id session variable for tenant isolation. Reference: Wiki/16-system-design.md Auth Boundary, ADR-012.
Acceptance Criteria:
- JWT payload includes: sub, org, roles (array of strings), tier (indie/professional/studio), iat, exp
- HTTP Gateway rejects requests without valid JWT with 401 (no service ever sees an invalid token)
- HTTP Gateway injects X-User-ID, X-Org-ID, X-Roles, X-Tier headers into all forwarded requests
- Integration test: writer role cannot call producer-only mutation; receives 403 from the service
- Postgres RLS: SELECT from a tenant table without setting app.org_id returns zero rows (not an error — RLS silently filters)
- gRPC internal calls include user context as metadata (x-user-id, x-org-id, x-roles)
Parent: Authentication Service

Title: Define all 7 RBAC roles and permission matrix and implement user_roles table
Description: Create services/auth/src/roles.ts defining the 7 roles: owner (full access including billing, member management), admin (full access except billing), writer (create/edit scripts, access AI features per consent), producer (approve breakdowns/schedules/budgets, manage call sheets), supervisor (on-set take logging, continuity), editor (editorial round-trip, OTIO/AAF), viewer (read-only access to scripts and production data). Create a permission matrix document at docs/rbac-matrix.md listing each role × resource × action. Add auth.user_org_roles table: user_id, org_id, role, granted_by, granted_at. A user can have different roles in different organisations.
Parent: As a platform engineer all API requests carry RBAC role claims so downstream services can enforce authorisation without querying the database

Title: Embed RBAC role array in JWT claims and update token signing service
Description: Update services/auth/src/jwt.service.ts signAccessToken() to: query auth.user_org_roles for the user + org combination, embed roles array in JWT payload, embed tier from auth.organizations. Update signAccessToken signature: signAccessToken(userId, orgId) → fetches roles from DB, embeds in token. Update /auth/me response to include roles array. Write test: user with writer role in org A and producer role in org B — JWT for org A login contains only [writer]; JWT for org B login contains only [producer]. Add roles to JWKS endpoint response documentation.
Parent: As a platform engineer all API requests carry RBAC role claims so downstream services can enforce authorisation without querying the database

Title: Create Postgres row-level security policy template for multi-tenant isolation
Description: Create packages/db/rls-template.sql documenting the RLS pattern used across all tenant tables. Pattern: (1) All tenant tables have org_id UUID NOT NULL column. (2) ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. (3) CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON ... USING (org_id = current_setting('app.org_id')::uuid). (4) Services set the session variable before queries: SET LOCAL app.org_id = $1. (5) Services use a Postgres role with BYPASSRLS=false (never use superuser in application). Write a Vitest test using a real Postgres (testcontainers) that verifies: user from org A cannot see org B data when RLS is active.
Parent: As a platform engineer all API requests carry RBAC role claims so downstream services can enforce authorisation without querying the database

Title: Implement Auth Service GraphQL subgraph with User and Organization types
Description: Create services/auth/src/graphql/schema.ts using graphql-tag and Apollo Server. Schema: type User { id: ID! email: String! name: String roles: [String!]! orgId: ID! tier: String! }. type Organization { id: ID! name: String! tier: String! memberCount: Int! }. Query: me: User (resolves from X-User-ID header — no DB call needed; data was embedded in JWT). Query: organization: Organization (resolves from X-Org-ID header; fetches from DB). Configure Apollo Federation directives (@key on User.id). Write Vitest tests for resolvers. Deploy updated Auth Service; verify query { me { id email roles } } works through Apollo Router.
Parent: As a platform engineer all API requests carry RBAC role claims so downstream services can enforce authorisation without querying the database

Title: Add Auth subgraph to Apollo Router supergraph and verify federated query execution
Description: Update apps/gateway/supergraph.yaml to include the Auth subgraph: name: auth, routing_url: http://auth-service/graphql. Run rover supergraph compose to generate the composed supergraph schema (apps/gateway/supergraph-schema.graphql). Update Apollo Router config (apps/gateway/router.yaml) to use the composed schema. Restart Apollo Router in staging. Verify: (1) introspection returns User and Organization types from Auth subgraph. (2) query { me { id email } } returns data for an authenticated user. (3) Invalid JWT on the request → Apollo Router returns 401 before reaching the subgraph.
Parent: As a platform engineer all API requests carry RBAC role claims so downstream services can enforce authorisation without querying the database

Title: As an enterprise IT admin I have a SCIM v2 endpoint to automate user provisioning and deprovisioning from Okta or Azure AD
Description: Enterprise studios use Okta or Azure AD to manage all software access. When an employee is onboarded the IT admin creates them in Okta and they should automatically appear in ScriptOS with the correct role. When they leave, deactivating them in Okta should immediately revoke ScriptOS access. This avoids manual access management at scale. The full SCIM implementation is deferred to Sprint 18 (GA Hardening) — this user story delivers a well-documented stub that returns 501 so the Okta integration team can begin configuration without blocking on ScriptOS implementation. Reference: Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 1 SCIM stub, Sprint 18 full implementation.
Acceptance Criteria:
- POST /auth/scim/v2/Users returns 501 Not Implemented with RFC 7644 compliant error JSON body: { schemas: [urn:ietf:params:scim:api:messages:2.0:Error], status: 501, detail: SCIM provisioning not yet implemented. Scheduled for Sprint 18. }
- GET /auth/scim/v2/Users returns 501 with same format
- OpenAPI specification for SCIM endpoints available at /auth/scim/v2/openapi.json
- Sprint 18 blocker ticket created referencing this stub
- SCIM endpoint is documented in the internal integration guide (docs/enterprise/scim.md)
Parent: Authentication Service

Title: Create SCIM v2 Users endpoint stub returning 501 Not Implemented
Description: Add route /auth/scim/v2/* to the Auth Service Express app. Implement: GET /auth/scim/v2/Users and POST /auth/scim/v2/Users both return HTTP 501 with Content-Type: application/scim+json and body { schemas: ['urn:ietf:params:scim:api:messages:2.0:Error'], status: '501', detail: 'SCIM provisioning is not yet implemented. Scheduled for Sprint 18 (GA Hardening). Contact engineering@scriptos.com for timeline.' }. Add middleware to log all SCIM endpoint hits to the audit log (including the caller IP and any SCIM headers) so we can track when enterprise customers start integrating before we've built it.
Parent: As an enterprise IT admin I have a SCIM v2 endpoint to automate user provisioning and deprovisioning from Okta or Azure AD

Title: Write OpenAPI spec and enterprise integration documentation for SCIM endpoints
Description: Create services/auth/openapi/scim.yaml as an OpenAPI 3.1 specification for the SCIM v2 /Users and /Groups endpoints (full spec — not just the stub). This documents what the full Sprint 18 implementation WILL support: SCIM User schema, group membership, filter queries, pagination, PUT for full replacement, PATCH for partial update (replace operations), DELETE for deprovisioning. Create docs/enterprise/scim.md: step-by-step guide for configuring Okta SCIM integration with ScriptOS, including screenshots of Okta provisioning settings and field mapping table.
Parent: As an enterprise IT admin I have a SCIM v2 endpoint to automate user provisioning and deprovisioning from Okta or Azure AD

Title: Shared Platform Packages
Description: Create the shared TypeScript and Protobuf packages that all 15 services import from. packages/ast (19 ASTNode types), packages/events (domain event envelope + payloads), packages/proto (gRPC .proto files + generated stubs), packages/common (logger, OTel, auth utils), packages/db (Atlas HCL schemas). These packages are the foundation of the entire service architecture — every service depends on at least two of them. They must be typed correctly from the start because changing these types later cascades across all services. Reference: Wiki/03-data-model.md (AST types), Wiki/16-system-design.md (package structure), Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 1.
Acceptance Criteria:
- packages/ast: tsc --noEmit passes; all 19 ASTNode types match wiki/03-data-model.md exactly
- packages/events: DomainEvent<T> envelope compiles; 5+ initial payload types defined
- packages/proto: buf generate produces TypeScript stubs with no errors; auth.proto complete; script.proto stubbed
- packages/common: logger, OTel init, and auth-header utilities importable from any service
- packages/db: Atlas HCL schema for auth tables applies cleanly to Postgres
- All packages importable via pnpm workspace protocol (@scriptos/ast, @scriptos/events, etc.)
- Zero TypeScript errors across all packages with strict mode enabled
Parent: Platform Foundation

Title: As a backend developer I have shared TypeScript packages for all domain types event schemas and gRPC stubs so every service starts with consistent data contracts
Description: Without shared packages every service would define its own ASTNode type, its own event payload shape, its own gRPC client. Inconsistencies accumulate and cross-service calls break silently. Shared packages enforce a single source of truth. The packages/ast package in particular is critical — it defines the 19-node Script AST that is the core domain object. Any mistake here propagates everywhere. The proto stubs eliminate hand-rolled gRPC clients. The events package ensures the publisher and consumer agree on payload shape at compile time. Reference: Wiki/03-data-model.md, Wiki/16-system-design.md, ADR-017 (monorepo).
Acceptance Criteria:
- packages/ast/index.ts exports 19 named ASTNode type variants matching the union type hierarchy in wiki/03-data-model.md: Project, Script, Episode, Act, Scene, SceneHeading, Action, Character, Dialogue, Parenthetical, Transition, Shot, TitlePage, MoreContinued, DualDialogue, OmittedScene, ContinuedBottom, Section, Synopsis
- RevisionColor enum exports 13 named colors (White, Blue, Pink, Yellow, Green, Goldenrod, Buff, Salmon, Cherry, Tan, Sage, DoubleWhite, DoubleBlue) matching WGA revision spec
- packages/events exports DomainEvent<T> with eventId (UUID v7), timestamp (ISO8601), version (number), subject (string), payload (T); at least 5 payload types defined
- buf generate succeeds on packages/proto producing TypeScript + gRPC-Web stubs; auth.proto complete with User and AuthService; script.proto has ScriptService stub
- packages/common Pino logger factory produces JSON logs with trace_id and span_id from OTel context
- packages/db Atlas project initialised; atlas schema apply against empty Postgres creates all auth.* tables
Parent: Shared Platform Packages

Title: Create packages/ast with all 19 ASTNode union types and RevisionColor enum per data model spec
Description: Create packages/ast/src/nodes.ts. Define individual TypeScript interfaces for each node type: each has id (string — UUID v7), type (literal string discriminant), parentId (string | null), position (number — fractional index), createdAt (string), updatedAt (string), plus node-specific fields. Specific fields per type from wiki/03-data-model.md: Scene (sceneNumber, intExt, location, timeOfDay, status, revisionColor, pageCount), SceneHeading (text), Action (text, isContinued), Character (name, extension, isDual), Dialogue (text), Parenthetical (text), Transition (text), Shot (text), etc. Export ASTNode as the discriminated union type of all 19 variants. Export isScene(node), isDialogue(node) type guards.
Parent: As a backend developer I have shared TypeScript packages for all domain types event schemas and gRPC stubs so every service starts with consistent data contracts

Create packages/events/src/payloads.ts: export ScriptCheckpointedPayload { scriptId, orgId, changedNodeIds, checkpointedAt }, BreakdownUpdatedPayload { scriptId, sceneId, changes }, ApprovalRequestedPayload { workflowId, scriptId, stepName, approverId, dueAt }, TakeSyncedPayload { sceneId, takeId, takeNumber, isCircled, syncedAt }, AssertionSyncedPayload { assertionId, sceneId, subject, text, syncedAt }. Create packages/events/src/subjects.ts: export NATS_SUBJECTS constant map — all subject strings used in the platform (script.checkpointed, breakdown.updated, approval.requested, supervisor.take.synced, supervisor.assertion.synced).

Write script.proto stub: ScriptService { rpc LockDraft(LockDraftRequest) returns (LockDraftResponse); rpc UnlockDraft...; rpc CheckpointCRDT...; rpc GetSceneSnapshot...; } — mark all methods with (-- to be implemented in Sprint 2). Export shutdown() for graceful shutdown. Create packages/db/src/pg.ts: export createPool(connectionString) returning a node-postgres Pool with: max: 20, idle timeout: 30s, connection timeout: 5s, SSL required (except in local dev). Title: Verify all shared packages compile and are importable across the workspace with strict TypeScript mode
Description: Create packages/ast/tsconfig.json, packages/events/tsconfig.json, packages/proto/tsconfig.json, packages/common/tsconfig.json, packages/db/tsconfig.json — all extending a root tsconfig.base.json with strict: true, noUncheckedIndexedAccess: true, exactOptionalPropertyTypes: true. Run pnpm turbo typecheck across all packages. Fix any strict-mode errors. Create a test service stub at services/script/src/test-imports.ts that imports from all 5 packages and uses at least one type from each — verifying workspace resolution works. Run pnpm turbo build to verify compiled output is produced. Verify buf generate still runs cleanly after tsconfig changes.
Parent: As a backend developer I have shared TypeScript packages for all domain types event schemas and gRPC stubs so every service starts with consistent data contracts

Title: API Gateway and Web App Foundation
Description: Deploy the HTTP GraphQL gateway (Apollo Router) and WebSocket gateway, then create the React web app scaffold with authentication flow. The gateways are the single entry point for all client traffic. Apollo Router federates subgraphs from all 15 services into a unified GraphQL API. The WebSocket gateway handles real-time collaboration WebSocket connections (CRDT sync). The React app provides the client shell: routing, auth state, and a working login flow. Reference: Wiki/16-system-design.md (Gateways, ADR-010, ADR-015), Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 1.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Apollo Router running in staging; query { me { id email } } returns authenticated user data
- JWT validation at Apollo Router: missing or invalid JWT returns 401 before request reaches any subgraph
- WebSocket gateway accepts connections and validates JWT on handshake; invalid JWT closes connection with 4001 code
- React web app compiles and serves; login page renders; Google OAuth redirects correctly
- Authenticated users see the app shell; unauthenticated users are redirected to /login
- Apollo Client in the React app sends Bearer token header with every GraphQL request
Parent: Platform Foundation

Title: As a developer all client API requests are routed through a federated GraphQL gateway with JWT validation and all WebSocket connections are authenticated
Description: The HTTP gateway is the security boundary for the entire platform. No service should ever be reachable directly from the public internet — all traffic flows through Apollo Router. This centralises JWT validation (no per-service auth logic), header injection, rate limiting, and observability. The WebSocket gateway is a separate process because WebSocket upgrade and long-lived connections have different scaling characteristics than HTTP request/response. Both gateways must enforce JWT before any business logic runs. Reference: ADR-010 (GraphQL Federation), ADR-015 (Dual Gateway), Wiki/16-system-design.md.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Apollo Router deployed to GKE; Istio routes external HTTPS traffic to Apollo Router only
- Apollo Router validates RS256 JWT using JWKS from Auth Service /auth/.well-known/jwks.json; caches public key with 5-minute TTL
- Valid JWT → X-User-ID, X-Org-ID, X-Roles, X-Tier headers injected into forwarded request
- Invalid JWT → 401 returned immediately; subgraph never receives the request
- WebSocket gateway accepts WS upgrade on /collaboration; validates JWT from Sec-WebSocket-Protocol header (token:<jwt>); closes connection with code 4001 if invalid
- Apollo Router health endpoint /health returns 200 so Kubernetes liveness probe works
- Apollo Router request/response trace spans visible in Grafana Jaeger
Parent: API Gateway and Web App Foundation

Title: Write Apollo Router configuration with Auth subgraph JWT validation plugin and header injection
Description: Create apps/gateway/router.yaml. Configuration sections: (1) supergraph: path: supergraph-schema.graphql. (2) homepage: false (production). (3) health_check: listen: 0.0.0.0:8088, path: /health. (4) authentication.router.jwt: jwks.url pointing to Auth Service JWKS endpoint, jwks.poll_interval: 5m, map_claims: sub→x-user-id, iss→(validate), exp→(validate). (5) headers propagation: send X-User-ID, X-Org-ID, X-Roles, X-Tier to all subgraphs. (6) telemetry: exporter.otlp.endpoint from env. Write apps/gateway/supergraph.yaml listing Auth subgraph. Run rover supergraph compose. Create Docker image for the gateway (apps/gateway/Dockerfile).
Parent: As a developer all client API requests are routed through a federated GraphQL gateway with JWT validation and all WebSocket connections are authenticated

Title: Configure X-User-ID X-Org-ID X-Roles and X-Tier header injection and propagation in Apollo Router
Description: In router.yaml headers section: configure request header propagation rules to inject enriched headers from JWT claims into all subgraph requests. Claims mapping: jwt.sub → X-User-ID (string), jwt.org → X-Org-ID (string), jwt.roles → X-Roles (comma-joined array), jwt.tier → X-Tier (string). Write integration test: send authenticated GraphQL request through Apollo Router (running in Docker Compose); introspect the headers received by a mock subgraph; verify all 4 headers are present and correct. Document this header contract in Wiki/16-system-design.md as the canonical inter-service auth pattern.
Parent: As a developer all client API requests are routed through a federated GraphQL gateway with JWT validation and all WebSocket connections are authenticated

Title: Deploy Apollo Router to GKE staging with Kubernetes Deployment and Service manifests
Description: Create infra/k8s/gateway/http/deployment.yaml: 2 replicas, image: apollo/router:<pinned version>, env from ExternalSecret (JWKS URL, Auth Service URL), resource limits (500m CPU, 256Mi RAM), readinessProbe on :8088/health, livenessProbe on :8088/health. Create infra/k8s/gateway/http/service.yaml: ClusterIP on port 4000. Create Istio Gateway + VirtualService routing external HTTPS traffic to Apollo Router service. Configure Istio to terminate TLS at the ingress gateway. Deploy to staging. Verify query { __typename } works from outside the cluster (via curl or browser). Add Apollo Router to CI release workflow.
Parent: As a developer all client API requests are routed through a federated GraphQL gateway with JWT validation and all WebSocket connections are authenticated

Title: Write WebSocket gateway stub server with JWT validation on handshake
Description: Create services/collaboration/src/ws-gateway.ts using the ws npm package. WS server listens on port 4001. On connection: (1) parse JWT from Sec-WebSocket-Protocol header value after token: prefix, (2) verify JWT using public key fetched from Auth Service JWKS, (3) if invalid: close connection with code 4001 and reason Invalid credentials, (4) if valid: log connection with userId + orgId, store in memory map (docId → Set<WebSocket>). On disconnect: remove from map. Message handler: echo message back (stub — real CRDT protocol in Sprint 3). Write Vitest test: connect with valid JWT → receives echo; connect with invalid JWT → closes with 4001.
Parent: As a developer all client API requests are routed through a federated GraphQL gateway with JWT validation and all WebSocket connections are authenticated

Title: Deploy WebSocket gateway to GKE staging with Kubernetes manifests
Description: Create infra/k8s/gateway/ws/deployment.yaml: 2 replicas, image from GCR, resource limits (500m CPU, 256Mi RAM), readinessProbe on HTTP /health (add /health endpoint to WS gateway: express server alongside ws server on port 4002). Create infra/k8s/gateway/ws/service.yaml: ClusterIP on port 4001. Create Istio VirtualService routing /collaboration/* traffic to WS gateway service (upgrade: websocket). Configure Istio to allow WebSocket upgrade. Test: wscat -c wss://staging.scriptos.com/collaboration with valid Bearer token in header — connection established and echo returned.
Parent: As a developer all client API requests are routed through a federated GraphQL gateway with JWT validation and all WebSocket connections are authenticated

Title: As a developer I can run the React web app locally see the login page and after authenticating be redirected to the app shell with my user data available
Description: The React app scaffold must be production-grade from the start: TypeScript strict mode, Tailwind CSS, React Router with protected routes, Apollo Client for GraphQL. The login flow is the first user interaction — it must work correctly with Google OAuth redirect. The auth context makes user data available to all components without prop drilling. This scaffold is reused for every subsequent frontend feature. Reference: Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 1 frontend tasks.
Acceptance Criteria:
- pnpm dev in apps/web starts Vite dev server on localhost:3000 in under 5 seconds
- /login page renders correctly with a Google Sign In button and ScriptOS branding
- Clicking Google Sign In redirects to Google OAuth; after consent redirects back to /auth/google/callback; JWT stored in httpOnly cookie; user redirected to /dashboard
- /dashboard (empty) renders for authenticated users; shows user email from useAuth() hook
- Unauthenticated access to /dashboard redirects to /login
- Apollo Client sends Authorization: Bearer <token> header with every GraphQL request
- TypeScript strict mode: zero ts errors in apps/web
Parent: API Gateway and Web App Foundation

Title: Create React web app scaffold with Vite React TypeScript Tailwind React Router and Apollo Client
Description: Create apps/web/ directory. Run: pnpm create vite apps/web --template react-ts. Install: tailwindcss, postcss, autoprefixer (@tailwindcss/vite plugin), react-router-dom@6, @apollo/client, graphql. Configure: tailwind.config.ts (content: src/**/*.{ts,tsx}), vite.config.ts (proxy /auth/* to Auth Service in dev mode, proxy /graphql to Apollo Router in dev mode). Configure tsconfig.json extending root tsconfig.base.json with strict: true. Create src/main.tsx: BrowserRouter wrapping ApolloProvider wrapping App. Create src/App.tsx with React Router routes: / (redirect to /dashboard), /login, /dashboard (protected). Add apps/web to Turborepo pipeline.
Parent: As a developer I can run the React web app locally see the login page and after authenticating be redirected to the app shell with my user data available

Title: Implement Google OAuth login page with redirect flow and callback handling
Description: Create src/pages/LoginPage.tsx: full-page centred layout (Tailwind), ScriptOS logo placeholder, tagline, Google Sign In button. Button click: redirect to /auth/google/login (proxied to Auth Service in dev; direct in production). Create src/pages/AuthCallbackPage.tsx: renders a loading spinner; on mount calls GET /auth/me with credentials: include (sends httpOnly cookie); on success dispatches auth state update and navigates to /dashboard; on error shows error message and link to /login. Add /auth/callback route in React Router pointing to AuthCallbackPage.
Parent: As a developer I can run the React web app locally see the login page and after authenticating be redirected to the app shell with my user data available

Title: Implement auth context useAuth hook and ProtectedRoute component
Description: Create src/context/AuthContext.tsx: AuthContext holds { user: User | null, isLoading: boolean, logout: () => void }. AuthProvider: on mount, calls GET /auth/me; on success sets user; on 401 sets user to null; sets isLoading false. logout(): calls POST /auth/logout, clears user state, navigates to /login. Create src/hooks/useAuth.ts: returns useContext(AuthContext); throws if used outside AuthProvider. Create src/components/ProtectedRoute.tsx: if isLoading shows Spinner; if user null redirects to /login with current path as state.redirect; else renders children. Wrap all non-public routes in ProtectedRoute.
Parent: As a developer I can run the React web app locally see the login page and after authenticating be redirected to the app shell with my user data available

Title: Configure Apollo Client with JWT authentication headers and automatic token refresh
Description: Create src/lib/apollo.ts. ApolloClient config: uri: /graphql (proxied in dev), HttpLink with credentials: include, authLink using setContext to read access token from memory (not localStorage — security) and add Authorization: Bearer header. Add TokenRefreshLink (from apollo-link-token-refresh): on 401 response, calls POST /auth/refresh, stores new access token in memory, retries original request. Create src/lib/token-store.ts: in-memory token storage (not localStorage) with getToken(), setToken(), clearToken() functions. InMemoryCache with type policies for Script and User types. Write Vitest + React Testing Library test: Apollo mock provider returns { me { id email } }; renders LoginPage; user sees their email after mock login.
Parent: As a developer I can run the React web app locally see the login page and after authenticating be redirected to the app shell with my user data available
